skill.name: mcp-integration
skill.description: Guides users through configuring Model Context Protocol servers for Claude Code plugins, including stdio, SSE, HTTP, WebSocket, authentication, tool naming, and testing patterns.
skill.summary: Configure MCP servers, authentication, and tool usage for Claude Code plugin integrations.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical combination of command execution, network access, and credential handling, but review found these patterns in documentation and example MCP configuration, not concealed executable code. The skill is not malicious, but it legitimately teaches users to start local processes, connect to remote MCP endpoints, and pass credentials through environment variables, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: Local MCP Server Process Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill documents stdio MCP servers that run local commands such as npx, Python modules, and plugin-bundled server paths. This is expected for MCP integration, but unsafe command paths or untrusted packages could execute code on the user machine.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The configuration explicitly launches local MCP server processes. The pattern is legitimate for MCP, but it is a real execution surface if users copy unsafe commands or packages.

medium_findings.title: Remote MCP Endpoint Connections
medium_findings.description: The examples and references configure SSE and HTTP MCP servers over remote URLs with authorization headers. This is normal MCP behavior, but endpoints can receive tool requests and credentials if users configure untrusted services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is directly shown in MCP examples and documentation. It is not malicious by itself, but endpoint trust is central to safe use.

medium_findings.title: Credential Forwarding Through Environment Variables
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to pass database URLs, API keys, passwords, and bearer tokens into MCP server configuration. The values are placeholders, not hardcoded secrets, but configured servers can access those credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references clearly pass credential-like environment variables and headers to MCP servers. The examples use placeholders, so this is an operational risk rather than evidence of credential theft.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Example URLs Are Documentation Placeholders
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged hardcoded URLs in examples and references. Review found example.com, hosted MCP service examples, and documentation links, not hidden exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are visible instructional examples and official documentation references. No evidence found that the skill itself sends data to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns in tool-usage.md and SKILL.md. Review found those matches in ordinary documentation words and examples, not crypto API calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations contain text such as descriptions or metadata, not MD5, DES, or other weak algorithm usage. This is a scanner token collision.

low_findings.title: Wildcard MCP Tool Allow Lists Require Care
low_findings.description: The documentation shows wildcard MCP tool allow lists but also warns to use them sparingly and prefer specific tools. Wildcards can grant broader access than needed if copied without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risk is real but mitigated by adjacent cautionary guidance. It remains a low concern because users may copy permissive patterns into plugins.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command-Based Stdio MCP Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: MCP stdio configuration starts local commands and can run plugin-bundled or package-manager-provided servers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly configure command and args fields for local process startup. This is expected functionality but should be reviewed before use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Values Passed To MCP Servers
dangerous_patterns.description: Environment variables and authorization headers are forwarded to MCP servers for database and API authentication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Credential placeholders are clearly present in environment and header examples. There is no hardcoded real secret, but the pattern has sensitive-data impact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network MCP Server Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: HTTP, SSE, and WebSocket MCP server types connect Claude Code plugins to remote services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Remote URL configuration is central to the documented server types. The risk depends on endpoint trust and transport security.

content.user_title: Configure MCP Servers for Claude Code
content.value_statement: Adding external tools to Claude Code plugins can be complex and error-prone. This skill provides practical MCP configuration, authentication, testing, and tool-usage guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains stdio, SSE, HTTP, and WebSocket MCP server configuration patterns.
Shows how to structure .mcp.json and plugin.json MCP entries.
Documents OAuth, bearer token, API key, and environment variable authentication options.
Describes MCP tool naming conventions for commands and agents.
Provides testing, debugging, and troubleshooting checklists for MCP integrations.
Highlights security practices for tokens, HTTPS, tool allow lists, and command paths.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a runnable MCP server implementation.
It cannot verify that a specific remote MCP endpoint is trustworthy.
It assumes users understand Claude Code plugin structure and local setup.
It does not replace a security review of third-party MCP servers or packages.

use_cases.title: Add a hosted service to a plugin
use_cases.description: Configure an SSE or HTTP MCP server with OAuth or token authentication for a cloud service.

use_cases.title: Expose local tools through MCP
use_cases.description: Set up a stdio MCP server that runs a local package, script, or database connector.

use_cases.title: Review MCP integration security
use_cases.description: Check token handling, URL choices, command paths, and allowed MCP tool scopes before publishing.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a server type
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the right MCP server type for my Claude Code plugin. I need to connect to [service or tool] and support [authentication method].

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic MCP configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft an MCP configuration for a Claude Code plugin that connects to [service]. Use [stdio, SSE, HTTP, or WebSocket] and list the required environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Wire MCP tools into commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Claude Code command that uses these MCP tools: [tool names]. Include validation steps, error handling, and a minimal allowed-tools list.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an MCP plugin before release
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this MCP plugin plan for security issues: [configuration summary]. Check command execution, endpoint trust, token handling, tool permissions, and user setup documentation.

output_examples.input: I need to connect a plugin to a project management service using OAuth.
output: Recommendation to use an SSE MCP server when the service supports hosted OAuth.
A checklist covering endpoint URL, OAuth scopes, first-use authentication, and tool naming.
Security notes about reviewing scopes and avoiding broad tool allow lists.

output_examples.input: I want a local database MCP server for a plugin command.
output: Guidance to use a stdio server with a validated command path and documented environment variables.
A setup checklist for database URL, credentials, debugging, and safe secret storage.
Warnings to avoid committing credentials and to restrict database permissions.

best_practices: Use HTTPS or WSS for remote MCP servers and verify endpoint ownership before sharing credentials.
Pass secrets through environment variables and document required variables without real values.
Pre-allow only the specific MCP tools each command needs.
anti_patterns: Do not copy wildcard MCP tool allow lists into commands without a clear need.
Do not run untrusted package-manager commands as stdio MCP servers.
Do not hardcode API tokens, database passwords, or bearer tokens in plugin files.

faq.question: Does this skill install an MCP server?
faq.answer: No. It provides configuration and integration guidance for MCP servers used by Claude Code plugins.

faq.question: Which MCP server types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers stdio, SSE, HTTP, and WebSocket server patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains OAuth, bearer tokens, API keys, environment variables, dynamic headers, and token handling practices.

faq.question: Is this safe for production plugin work?
faq.answer: Yes, with review. Users should verify endpoints, command paths, package sources, tool permissions, and secret handling.

faq.question: Does it support both Claude and Codex workflows?
faq.answer: The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code support, while the content focuses on Claude Code plugin MCP integration.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing a plugin?
faq.answer: Review remote URLs, OAuth scopes, environment variables, command execution paths, allowed tools, and setup documentation.
